chunk_summary: This section outlines a decision pathway for initiating ketogenic diet therapy (KDT) in pediatric drug-resistant epilepsy (DRE), emphasizing patient selection, contraindications, and monitoring outcomes over a 3-month trial period.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for initiating ketogenic diet therapy in pediatric drug-resistant epilepsy.
Identify contraindications and safety considerations before starting KDT.
Learn how to monitor and evaluate the efficacy and tolerability of KDT over a defined trial period.
Recognize region-specific adaptations for implementing KDT, such as in India.
key_facts: KDT is considered after failure of two appropriately chosen antiseizure medications and evaluation of treatable structural causes.
Contraindications include fatty acid oxidation defects, unexplained episodic lethargy with hypoglycemia, severe baseline acidosis, or inability to ensure hydration and monitoring.
Initial trial duration is 1–2 weeks for tolerability adjustments, with efficacy reassessment at ~3 months.
Clinically meaningful seizure reduction and acceptable growth/tolerance are required to continue KDT.
India-specific adaptations include simplifying food choices, avoiding carbohydrate-containing liquid medicines, and having an emergency escalation plan.
reference_quote: Definition of drug-resistant epilepsy: 'Failure of adequate trials of two tolerated, appropriately chosen and used antiseizure drug schedules to achieve sustained seizure freedom.' (Kwan et al, ILAE Task Force)
document_key_topics: Criteria for initiating ketogenic diet therapy
Contraindications and safety screening for KDT
